early_readers.description: Simple, repetitive sentences and vivid imagery to captivate the youngest readers.
examples: Look at the bunny. The bunny is hopping.
The cat says 'Meow.' The dog says 'Woof!'
suggestions: Use bright and engaging imagery with large text.
Include onomatopoeia and repetitive phrases for emphasis.
Keep sentences short and easy to follow.

young_children.description: Engaging dialogue, playful rhymes, and simple plots that delight and educate.
examples: Hi, I’m Ava! What’s your name?
Up, up goes the kite, flying high into the night!
suggestions: Introduce basic dialogue and rhyming patterns.
Focus on relatable adventures and gentle lessons.
Encourage interaction through questions or prompts.

tweens.description: Adventurous storylines with relatable characters and humorous dialogue.
examples: Zara raced through the forest, her heart pounding like a drum.
Why did you take the map, Jake? Now we're totally lost!
suggestions: Add light humor and character-driven dialogue.
Balance action with descriptive moments to build suspense.

teens.description: Complex narratives with emotional depth and thought-provoking themes.
examples: Lila stared at the city lights, wondering where she truly belonged.
‘It’s not about winning,’ he said. ‘It’s about standing for what’s right.’
suggestions: Explore nuanced relationships and identity struggles.
Use descriptive prose to create immersive settings.
Introduce moral dilemmas or significant personal growth arcs.

family_readers.description: Heartwarming stories with universal themes that bring all ages together.
examples: The family gathered around the campfire, sharing stories under the stars.
‘Love is like a quilt,’ Grandma said. ‘It’s stitched together with memories.’
suggestions: Focus on themes of connection, kindness, and shared experiences.
Use nostalgic imagery to appeal to older readers.
Encourage discussion or reflection through open-ended questions.

general.description: Flexible writing styles that can adapt to any audience or tone.
examples: Once upon a time, in a land far away, there was a magical tree.
The wind whispered secrets to the curious child.
suggestions: Blend simple language with imaginative settings.
Use storytelling techniques that suit a wide range of readers.
Experiment with tone and pacing based on the audience.
